title: Lower Bounds on Lyapunov Exponents for Linear PDEs Driven by Stochastic Navier-Stokes
speaker: Samuel Punshon-Smith
abstract:
I will present recent work with Hairer, Rosati and Yi establishing quantitative lower bounds for the top Lyapunov exponent of linear PDEs driven by two-dimensional stochastic Navier-Stokes equations on the torus. For both the advection-diffusion equation and the linearized Navier-Stokes equations, we prove that the top Lyapunov exponent is bounded below by a negative power of the diffusion parameter κ, showing that the decay rate cannot be super-exponential.
This result provides the first rigorous lower bound on the Batchelor scale in terms of diffusivity, partially answering a conjecture of Doering and Miles. Our approach introduces the concept of "high-frequency stochastic instability," demonstrating that high-frequency states are unstable under stochastic perturbations. This leads to a Lyapunov drift condition for the H1
 norm over the L2
 norm and quantitative estimates on the decay rate in the diffusivity parameter.
I will discuss the implications of our results for understanding mixing phenomena in fluid dynamics.

proof_stages.label: Stochastic-fluid-driven linear cocycle setup
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes a two-dimensional periodic setting, takes the driving velocity to solve nondegenerately forced stochastic Navier-Stokes, and identifies the main linear PDEs under study, especially advection-diffusion and linearized Euler/Navier-Stokes.

proof_stages.label: Top Lyapunov exponent via multiplicative ergodic theory
proof_stages.summary: For each fixed linear equation the top Lyapunov exponent is defined as the long-time exponential growth or decay rate, with the multiplicative ergodic theorem supplying existence and independence from the initial velocity/noise realization, though not from the operator or forcing law.

proof_stages.label: Quantitative lower-bound theorem and motivation
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem gives a polynomial-in-diffusivity lower bound on the top Lyapunov exponent, implying non-super-exponential decay and relating the problem to Batchelor-scale mixing heuristics and the conjectured uniform-in-diffusivity bound.

proof_stages.label: Projectivization and Furstenberg-Khasminskii reduction
proof_stages.summary: By normalizing the solution in L2 one obtains a projective Markov process for which the Lyapunov exponent can be bounded by a stationary average of an H1 energy, reducing the theorem to existence of a stationary measure with finite H1 moments.

proof_stages.label: Uniform H1 Lyapunov-function estimate
proof_stages.summary: The required invariant-measure control is obtained from a strong regularization/Lyapunov estimate bounding the expected H1 norm of the projected process uniformly in the initial condition, despite the lack of compactness and deterministic parabolic estimates being insufficient.

proof_stages.label: High-frequency stochastic instability and spectral median
proof_stages.summary: To prove the Lyapunov estimate, the argument introduces high-frequency stochastic instability and the spectral median as a way to quantify whether normalized mass is escaping to high Fourier modes.

proof_stages.label: Short-time creation of low modes
proof_stages.summary: The core probabilistic input is that, starting with large spectral median, nondegenerate stochastic forcing populates low Fourier modes with high probability on slightly super-diffusive time scales, proved by a short-time Wiener-chaos/Duhamel expansion where the leading Gaussian term dominates the higher-order terms.

proof_stages.label: Downward spectral-median drift and Markov-chain completion
proof_stages.summary: The low-mode lower bound is combined with rapid diffusive damping of high frequencies to force the spectral median to drop with high probability, and iterating this drift as a Markov-chain estimate yields the uniform H1 moment bound and hence the Lyapunov-exponent lower bound.

examples.name: Degenerate zero operator
examples.note: The trivial choice L=0 is mentioned during a clarification to show that the Lyapunov exponent cannot be independent of the chosen linear operator.
examples.engagement_reason: The zero operator is only invoked hypothetically to note that the Lyapunov exponent clearly depends on the choice of operator.

passes.reason: The zero operator is the trivial collapsed operator and is invoked to refute independence of the Lyapunov exponent from L.

examples.name: Arbitrary bounded Lipschitz velocity field
examples.note: A deterministic bounded Lipschitz advecting field is used as a comparison case where ruling out super-exponential decay remains open, highlighting what randomness buys in the theorem.

examples.name: Normalized heat equation on the unit sphere
examples.note: The normalized heat equation and its Laplacian eigenfunctions illustrate why high-frequency states are unstable to low-frequency perturbations, after the instability mechanism has already been stated.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker writes the normalized heat equation, identifies Laplacian eigenfunctions as steady states, and reasons through their stability on the unit sphere.

passes.reason: The normalized heat flow is the pure-diffusion/no-advection limiting projective dynamics, used as a toy illustration of high-frequency instability.
